text.title: Quarterly Retainer Review Script Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every quarterly retainer review must enforce to convert review-time into renewal-time.

rule.statement: The call is 45 minutes. Hard stop. Segments: data 10 min, outcomes 15 min, blockers 10 min, renewal 10 min. If you run past, you've lost the renewal momentum.
rule.rationale: Gainsight 2024 customer-success data: QBRs over 60 minutes correlate with lower renewal rates (-12 pp) because they drift into advocacy/complaint mode. 45 min forces preparation density and signals respect for client time.

rule.statement: The call ends with one of four explicit decisions named aloud by the client: (1) continue same terms, (2) scale up scope, (3) scope down, (4) terminate. "Let me think about it" is not a decision — push for at least a date by which they will name one.

rationale.source: Chris Voss, Never Split the Difference (2016), ch. 7 - 'no' as decision

rule.rationale: Open-ended endings produce churn invisible until the renewal date passes. Forcing a named decision (or a named date) makes the next step actionable. The four-option frame anchors the conversation away from binary "yes/no" stress.

rule.statement: The first 10 min is data-only: hours used / hours bought, SLA % met, scope items shipped vs. agreed. No interpretation, no spin. Numbers visible on screen the whole time.

rationale.source: Kahneman, Thinking Fast and Slow - anchoring effect

rule.rationale: Numbers shown first anchor the client's reference frame in objective territory. Narrative-first reviews let the client anchor on emotion (last bad week) instead of quarter trend. Data must precede story.

rule.statement: If the decision is "scale up" or "scope down", the exact scope delta is written into the decision-record JSON live during the call. Hours, deliverable types, SLA target. Verbal-only changes don't bind.

rationale.source: Statement-of-work / change-order discipline; Mike Monteiro, Design Is a Job (2012), ch. 5

rule.rationale: Verbal scope changes are the #1 source of next-quarter conflict in service businesses. Writing them live makes both parties read them; client confirmation in real time is the contract amendment.

rule.statement: A 1-page email follow-up arrives within 24h of the call: decision, scope delta, next-quarter dates, action items per side. If you can't send it in 24h, your call was too long.
rule.rationale: HubSpot 2024 data: client-signed contract amendments arrive 3x faster when follow-up is sent within 24h vs. 48h+. Cognitive recall decays past 24h; the email is the binding artifact.

example.title: Decision-record after a 45-min QBR
example.code: { "client": "Acme Inc.", "quarter": "Q2-2026", "decision": "scale_up", "scope_delta": { "hours_bought_per_month_from": 40, "hours_bought_per_month_to": 60, "new_deliverables": ["weekly content audit", "monthly SEO report"], "sla_target_response_hours": 4 }, "effective_date": "2026-07-01", "next_review_date": "2026-10-15", "client_decision_maker": "M. Chen (CMO)", "agency_lead": "yourname", "follow_up_email_sent_at": "2026-04-16T18:00:00Z" }
example.note: Illustrates r2 (named decision), r4 (scope delta written), r5 (follow-up timestamp captured).

example.title: Open-ended exit
example.code:
Client at minute 47: "We're really happy with the work. Let me sync with finance and get back to you." Agency lead: "Sure, no problem!"
Problem: violates r2 (no named decision, no date). 6 weeks later client renews at original scope minus $1k/month "due to budget constraints." Repair: at minute 42 — "Totally. To plan capacity my side, can we put a date on the finance sync? Even a Slack reply by Friday works."
example.note: Illustrates r1 + r2 violation pattern that turns a renewal into a discount conversation later.
